q3: The main recurring issue was with closing sentences — the model had a tendency to bundle distinct ideas into a single clause, which diluted both points. It took explicit back-and-forth to get it to split the final two directions into separate sentences and order them by argumentative weight rather than by convenience.
q5: The rubric was genuinely well-aligned with what I care about — 'Paragraph-Level Orientation' and 'Closing Force' in particular map closely to things I find myself correcting in drafts. That said, I'm not sure the model was actively consulting it in real time so much as the rubric happened to codify preferences that surfaced through my feedback anyway. It helped, but I'd say it helped me articulate what I wanted as much as it helped the model deliver it.
q6: Seeing the rubric laid out made me notice that the model was consistently stronger on argumentative structure at the paragraph level than on closing payoffs — it would orient a paragraph well and build through it cleanly, but then underdeliver at the end, which is exactly where 'Closing Force' is supposed to catch it. Without the rubric framing it explicitly, I might have just kept saying 'the ending feels weak' without identifying it as a systematic pattern.

q3: The model had a recurring tendency to over-explain the pronoun referent issue I flagged — it understood the symptom but kept proposing fixes that were slightly too cautious, trading one kind of awkwardness for another. It also occasionally bundled what should have been two distinct closing beats into a single trailing clause, which is exactly the kind of thing I find frustrating.
q5: The rubric captured a lot of what I care about, particularly the four-beat paragraph rhythm and the closing force criteria — those are genuinely central to how I think about methods writing. What it didn't quite capture is the difference between a transition that sequences and one that creates logical dependency; the rubric gestures at reorderability but doesn't make the dependency relationship feel as urgent as it should.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was consistently stronger on paragraph openings than on paragraph closings — it got the orientation right more reliably than it landed the 'what this buys us' beat. Without the rubric making that distinction explicit, I might have just felt vaguely unsatisfied with the endings without pinpointing why.

q3: The ending was the persistent sticking point — the model kept defaulting to a cautious, hedged close even when the argument had already earned a definitive conclusion. It took explicit feedback across multiple rounds to get a landing that felt like an arrival rather than a retreat.
q5: I did look at it, and it captured several things I care about quite precisely — especially the four-beat paragraph rhythm and the point about closing force. What it got slightly wrong was the framing around hedges: it distinguished between 'filler hedges' and 'honest uncertainty hedges' correctly in principle, but the model still struggled to apply that distinction in practice, which suggests the rubric was more useful as a description than as actionable guidance.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model's closing-sentence problem wasn't random — it was systematic. The rubric explicitly addresses closing force, and yet the model still defaulted to soft landings, which tells me the rubric was being used more as a checklist than as a genuine constraint on generation. I wouldn't have framed it that way without seeing the rubric alongside the drafts.
